timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Ezekiel

sections.header: Verses 1-5 — Sent to a Rebellious House

heb.paragraph: God addresses Ezekiel as ben-adam ('son of man' / 'mortal') 93 times in the book — more than any other title. It emphasises Ezekiel's humanity and mortality in contrast to the divine glory he has just witnessed. The title stresses the infinite gap between the holy God and his human messenger.

A wordplay on 'house of Israel' (bet yisra'el). God redefines the nation by its character rather than its covenant name — they are rebels, not merely wanderers. This phrase occurs 12 times in Ezekiel, a relentless rhetorical hammer.

panels.ctx: The commission follows the standard prophetic call pattern: theophany (ch. 1), commission (2:1-5), objection (implicit — Ezekiel is told not to fear), and sign (the scroll, 2:9-3:3). But Ezekiel's call is uniquely harsh: God warns from the start that the people will not listen. Success is not measured by response but by faithfulness to deliver the message.

cross.note: Isaiah's commission also included the warning that the people would hear but not understand — prophetic calling does not guarantee audience receptivity.
Jesus sends disciples with a similar warning: shake the dust off your feet if they refuse to listen.

notes.note: The Spirit entering Ezekiel and setting him on his feet demonstrates that prophetic ministry is impossible by human strength alone — the Spirit empowers what the flesh cannot do.
God calls Israel 'a rebellious nation' that has transgressed 'to this very day.' The rebellion is not merely past but ongoing and present.
Whether they listen or not — God's word must still be proclaimed. The prophet is accountable for delivery, not results.
Calvin observes that the Spirit raises Ezekiel because the prophet was prostrate before the divine glory. Human beings cannot stand before God by their own power — they need to be lifted up by grace.
Calvin stresses the 'whether they listen or fail to listen' formula: ministers must preach faithfully regardless of audience response. Results belong to God.
Ben-adam is simply 'human being' or 'mortal' in Hebrew, not a messianic title. It emphasises Ezekiel's creatureliness over against the Creator's glory.
The term goyim ('nations') applied to Israel is deliberately shocking — God classifies his own covenant people with the pagan nations they were supposed to be distinct from.

Block argues the repeated 'rebellious house' (bet meri) is not merely descriptive but performative — God is formally charging Israel in covenantal lawsuit language. The prophet is commissioned as a covenant prosecutor, not just a messenger.

Zimmerli identifies the 'recognition formula' embedded in v.5: 'they will know that a prophet has been among them.' This is the first instance of a pattern that will recur throughout the book — God's actions are designed to produce knowledge of who YHWH is.

sections.header: Verses 6-10 — The Scroll of Lament, Mourning, and Woe

heb.paragraph: The scroll written on both sides is unusual — scrolls normally had writing only on the inside. Both sides filled with lament, mourning, and woe indicate the overwhelming fullness of God's message of judgment. There is no room for additions; the judgment is complete and comprehensive.

panels.ctx: The scroll symbolism is profound: Ezekiel must literally internalize God's word before he can proclaim it. Unlike Jeremiah, who resisted his calling, Ezekiel is commanded to eat and then speak. The message of judgment must become part of the prophet before it can become a word to the people. This anticipates the prophetic sign-acts of chapters 4-5.

cross.note: Jeremiah also consumed God's words: 'When your words came, I ate them; they were my joy.' But Ezekiel's scroll is filled with woe, not joy.
John is told to eat a scroll that is sweet in his mouth but bitter in his stomach — directly echoing Ezekiel's experience.

notes.note: Briers, thorns, and scorpions are metaphors for the hostile reception Ezekiel will face. But God commands him not to fear — divine commission overrides human intimidation.
Writing on both sides of the scroll emphasises completeness. The three words — lament, mourning, woe — capture the totality of the coming judgment.
Calvin sees the scroll as a demonstration that God's word is fixed and predetermined. The prophet does not invent the message; he receives it complete and delivers it whole.
The Hebrew qinah ('lament/dirge') is a technical term for funeral poetry, previewing the dirges Ezekiel will compose over Tyre (ch. 27) and Egypt (ch. 32).
Block sees the scroll-eating as a priestly act of consecration — just as a priest ingests the sacred, Ezekiel ingests the divine word. His body becomes the vessel of revelation, a living scroll.

Zimmerli notes that the scroll written on both sides (panim ve'ahor) recalls the tablets of the law written on both sides (Exod 32:15). Ezekiel's scroll is a new Sinai revelation for the exile generation.

rows.label: vv.1-5
rows.text: Commission: Sent to a Rebellious House

rows.label: vv.8-10
rows.text: The Scroll of Lament and Woe

lit.note: Classic prophetic commission pattern: divine address > charge > warning > symbolic equipment (the scroll).

themes.note: Prophecy and Mission both peak at 9: this is the commissioning of the prophet. Judgment at 8: the scroll is filled with woe.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: 1), commission (2:1-5), objection (implicit — Ezekiel is told not to fear), and sign (the scroll, 2:9-3:3)…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Call and the Scroll

translations.text: Then I looked, and I saw a hand stretched out to me. In it was a scroll,
And when I looked, behold, a hand was stretched out to me, and behold, a scroll of a book was in it.

And you, son of man, do not be afraid of them or their words. Do not be afraid, though briers and thorns are all around you and you live among scorpions. Do not be afraid of what they say or be terrified by them, though they are a rebellious people.

And you, son of man, be not afraid of them, nor be afraid of their words, though briers and thorns are with you and you sit on scorpions. Be not afraid of their words, nor be dismayed at their looks, for they are a rebellious house.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "The Spirit entering Ezekiel and setting him on his feet demonstrates that prophetic ministry is impossible by human strength alone — the Spirit empowers what the flesh cannot do."
